Battery Carbon Footprint Declaration: EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542 DPP Requirements

The European Union's commitment to a sustainable and circular economy has culminated in the landmark Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 concerning batteries and waste batteries, which repeals the previous Directive 2006/66/EC. This comprehensive legislative framework aims to govern the entire lifecycle of batteries placed on the EU market, from design and production to reuse and recycling. A pivotal aspect of this regulation, particularly under Article 7, is the mandatory Carbon Footprint Declaration, designed to enhance transparency and reduce the environmental impact of batteries across their value chain [1] [2].

This technical guide delves into the intricacies of the EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542's carbon footprint declaration requirements. It provides a detailed overview of the scope and timeline, identifies the battery categories affected, elucidates the carbon footprint calculation methodology based on Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) principles, explains the performance classification system, and outlines the essential data fields for the Battery Passport. Furthermore, it explores the role of SHA-256 hashing in ensuring data integrity and considers the significant impact on global battery manufacturers and raw material exporters, including those from South Africa dealing in platinum group metals (PGMs) and lithium.

1. The EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542: Scope and Timeline

Regulation (EU) 2023/1542, which officially entered into force on 17 August 2023, represents a monumental shift in the regulatory landscape for batteries. Its applicability extends to all batteries that are either placed on the EU market or put into service within the European Union, a crucial point being that this applies irrespective of their geographical origin [3]. This expansive scope is deliberately designed to ensure a level playing field, fostering fair competition among all economic operators involved in the battery supply chain, including manufacturers, importers, and distributors, whether they are based within the EU or in third countries [3]. The regulation meticulously categorizes and covers a diverse array of battery types, including but not limited to portable batteries (commonly found in consumer electronics), Starting, Lighting and Ignition (SLI) batteries (primarily used in vehicles), Light Means of Transport (LMT) batteries (such as those powering e-bikes and e-scooters), Electric Vehicle (EV) batteries (for automotive applications), and various industrial batteries [4].

The implementation of this new regulatory framework is structured as a phased transition, with various provisions and requirements progressively coming into effect over an extended period, ultimately reaching full implementation by 2036. A significant milestone in this transition is the complete repeal of the former Directive 2006/66/EC, which is scheduled for 18 August 2025. From this date forward, Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 will stand as the sole and definitive legal instrument governing batteries within the European Union, thereby eliminating previous inconsistencies and providing a harmonized regulatory environment [4]. This phased approach is intended to provide economic operators with sufficient time to adapt their production processes, supply chain management, and data reporting systems to align with the new, more stringent standards.

2. Carbon Footprint Declaration Requirements (Article 7)

Article 7 of the EU Battery Regulation stands as a central pillar of the legislation, mandating a comprehensive carbon footprint declaration for specific battery categories. This requirement is not merely an administrative burden but a strategic imperative, forming a cornerstone of the EU's broader strategy to actively promote the adoption of low-carbon materials and advanced technologies throughout the battery value chain. By doing so, it directly supports the overarching transition towards a more sustainable and circular economy within the Union [2].

2.1. Affected Battery Categories

The carbon footprint declaration requirements are precisely delineated and specifically target three primary battery categories, reflecting their significant environmental impact and strategic importance within the EU's sustainability agenda [2] [5]:

  • Electric Vehicle (EV) batteries: These are batteries specifically designed to power a wide range of electric vehicles, including passenger cars, buses, and heavy-duty transport vehicles. Given the rapid electrification of the transport sector, these batteries represent a substantial and growing portion of the market.
  • Rechargeable industrial batteries with a capacity exceeding 2 kWh: This broad category encompasses batteries utilized in diverse industrial applications, such as large-scale energy storage systems, critical communication infrastructure, and heavy industrial equipment. Their substantial energy capacity often correlates with a larger environmental footprint, necessitating stringent oversight.
  • Light Means of Transport (LMT) batteries: This category includes batteries used in emerging urban mobility solutions, such as electric bicycles (e-bikes) and electric scooters (e-scooters). While individually smaller, their collective proliferation warrants inclusion in carbon footprint declarations.

It is important to note that portable batteries (typically weighing under 5kg and commonly found in consumer electronics) and SLI (Starting, Lighting, and Ignition) batteries are currently exempt from the mandatory carbon footprint declaration requirements. However, the European Commission has a clear mandate to assess the technical and economic feasibility of extending these requirements to portable batteries by 31 December 2030, indicating a potential future expansion of the regulation's scope [2] [5]. This phased approach allows for focused implementation on the most impactful categories first, while preparing for broader coverage as technology and market conditions evolve.

2.2. Content of the Carbon Footprint Declaration

Manufacturers are obligated to provide a detailed carbon footprint declaration for each distinct battery model produced at each individual manufacturing plant. This declaration is not a standalone document but an integral component of the technical documentation required for the EU declaration of conformity. Its contents must be comprehensive and include the following key elements [2] [5]:

  • Administrative information about the manufacturer: This includes the legal name, address, and contact details of the entity responsible for placing the battery on the market.
  • The battery model: A clear and unambiguous identification of the specific battery model to which the declaration pertains.
  • The geographical location of the manufacturing plant: Precise details of where the battery was produced, ensuring traceability to its origin.
  • The identification number of the EU declaration of conformity of the battery: A unique identifier linking the carbon footprint declaration to the broader conformity assessment documentation.
  • A web link providing access to a public version of the study supporting the carbon footprint values: This ensures transparency and allows stakeholders to review the underlying data and methodology used to derive the declared carbon footprint.
  • The carbon footprint expressed in kilograms of carbon dioxide equivalent (kg CO2e) per kilowatt-hour (kWh) of total energy provided by the battery over its expected service life: This is the core quantitative metric, providing a standardized measure of the battery's environmental impact. Crucially, this figure must be broken down by each distinct lifecycle stage, offering granular insight into emission sources.

A transitional provision stipulates that until the carbon footprint information becomes digitally accessible via the QR code integrated into the Battery Passport (a requirement anticipated to be fully operational by 18 February 2027), the physical declaration must accompany the battery. This ensures that critical environmental information is available to relevant parties even before the full digital infrastructure is in place [2] [5].

3. Carbon Footprint Calculation Methodology: Lifecycle Assessment (LCA) per ISO 14040/14044

The EU Battery Regulation mandates a highly rigorous and standardized methodology for calculating the carbon footprint of batteries. This approach is primarily anchored in the internationally recognized Product Environmental Footprint (PEF) methodology, which itself is deeply rooted in comprehensive Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) principles. This ensures consistency and comparability across different battery products and manufacturers. The PEF methodology aligns seamlessly with established international standards, specifically ISO 14040 (Environmental management – Life cycle assessment – Principles and framework) and ISO 14044 (Environmental management – Life cycle assessment – Requirements and guidelines), as well as ISO 14067 (Carbon footprint of products) [5] [6]. To facilitate precise and harmonized calculations, the European Commission's Joint Research Centre (JRC) has been instrumental in developing specific calculation rules tailored for various battery types. This began with detailed guidelines for EV batteries and industrial batteries, with the development of rules for LMT batteries currently underway [5].

3.1. System Boundary and Lifecycle Stages

A fundamental aspect of the carbon footprint calculation is the clear definition of its system boundary. This boundary dictates which stages and processes of a battery's life cycle must be included in the assessment. The regulation explicitly requires the calculation to cover the battery's entire lifecycle, with the notable exclusion of the use phase. This approach typically follows either a cradle-to-grave or cradle-to-cradle model. The specific stages that must be meticulously accounted for in the carbon footprint assessment include [5]:

  • Raw material extraction and processing: This initial stage encompasses all activities related to the sourcing, mining, refining, and initial processing of critical raw materials essential for battery production. This includes, but is not limited to, lithium, cobalt, nickel, and natural graphite. The environmental impacts at this stage, such as energy consumption and land disturbance, are crucial for a holistic assessment.
  • Active material and cell manufacturing: This stage covers the energy-intensive processes involved in producing the core components of a battery cell, including the anode, cathode, electrolyte, and separator materials, as well as the assembly of the cell casing.
  • Battery assembly: This refers to the subsequent stages where individual battery cells are integrated into modules and then into complete battery packs at the manufacturing plant. This includes all associated manufacturing processes and energy inputs.
  • Distribution and transport: This stage accounts for the environmental impact of logistics, covering the transportation of raw materials, intermediate products, and finished batteries from their respective points of production to the final point of sale within the EU market.
  • End-of-life processing: This crucial final stage addresses the environmental impacts associated with the management of batteries at the end of their useful life. This includes processes such as collection, sorting, dismantling, recycling (to recover valuable materials), and the safe disposal of any remaining waste.

3.2. Functional Unit and Reference Flow

To ensure comparability and standardization across different battery types and applications, the carbon footprint is expressed per a precisely defined functional unit. This unit is established as one kilowatt-hour (kWh) of the total energy that the battery system is designed to provide over its entire expected service life. Complementing this, the reference flow is defined as the mass of the battery (measured in kilograms) required to fulfill this specified function. It is imperative that all quantitative input and output data collected by the manufacturer for the carbon footprint calculation are directly related to and normalized by this reference flow [5]. This ensures that comparisons are made on an equivalent basis of delivered energy, rather than simply by battery size or weight.

3.3. Data Requirements: Primary vs. Secondary

One of the most operationally demanding and critical aspects of the PEF methodology, as applied to batteries, is its stringent data tiering requirements. Primary (company-specific) data is unequivocally mandated for the main product production lifecycle stage. This means that manufacturers must collect and utilize site-specific, measured activity data directly from their manufacturing plants for the production of battery-specific components, including the anode, cathode, electrolyte, separator, and cell casing. The use of generic or industry-average data is explicitly prohibited for these core components, underscoring the need for granular, verifiable information. For all other lifecycle stages, secondary data (which can be sourced from third-party databases, industry averages, or proxy data) may be utilized [5]. This dual requirement necessitates the establishment of robust internal data collection systems within manufacturing facilities and demands close, transparent collaboration across the entire supply chain to ensure the availability and accuracy of both primary and secondary data. The integrity of the carbon footprint declaration hinges on the quality and specificity of this underlying data.

3.4. Third-Party Verification

To further ensure the credibility and reliability of the carbon footprint declarations, the regulation stipulates that the declared carbon footprint must undergo independent verification by a designated notified body. This verification process is an essential part of the battery's broader conformity assessment. Crucially, self-certification by manufacturers is not permitted, thereby reinforcing the EU's commitment to transparent, objective, and auditable environmental claims. This independent oversight adds a layer of assurance for regulators, consumers, and other stakeholders that the reported carbon footprint values are accurate and compliant with the regulatory framework [5].

4. Carbon Footprint Performance Classes (A through E)

Building upon the foundational requirement of carbon footprint declaration, the EU Battery Regulation introduces an innovative system of carbon footprint performance classes. This classification system is analogous to the familiar energy efficiency labels seen on appliances, providing a clear and standardized way to communicate a battery's environmental performance. Batteries will be graded into these distinct classes, typically ranging from A to E, with Class A representing batteries that exhibit the lowest environmental impact and are therefore considered the most sustainable [2] [5]. This system allows for easy comparison and incentivizes manufacturers to continuously improve the environmental performance of their products.

The European Commission is tasked with the ongoing responsibility of evaluating and, if necessary, adjusting the number of performance classes and their corresponding thresholds every three years. This dynamic review process ensures that the classification system remains relevant and responsive to technological advancements within the battery industry and evolving market realities. The ultimate objective of this phased approach is the establishment of stringent maximum lifecycle carbon footprint thresholds. Once these thresholds are officially set and come into effect, any battery that exceeds these prescribed limits will be prohibited from being placed on the EU market [2] [5]. This mechanism provides a powerful regulatory lever to drive down the overall carbon intensity of batteries available in the European Union.

5. The Battery Passport Data Model: What Fields are Required in the DPP

The Digital Battery Passport (DBP) represents a paradigm shift in product information management and traceability. Mandated to become fully operational from 18 February 2027 for Electric Vehicle (EV) batteries and industrial rechargeable batteries with a capacity exceeding 2 kWh, the DBP is conceived as a secure, comprehensive digital record. This record will be readily accessible via a QR code affixed to the battery, providing a direct link to a wealth of structured, accessible, and continuously updatable information. This data will encompass the battery’s fundamental identity, its precise chemical composition, detailed performance characteristics, crucial sustainability attributes, and essential guidelines for its end-of-life handling [3] [7]. The overarching aim of the Battery Passport is to significantly enhance transparency, improve traceability throughout the entire battery value chain, and bolster the sustainability credentials of batteries. It serves as a unified framework, enabling all relevant stakeholders—from manufacturers and regulators to recyclers and consumers—to access and utilize critical battery-related data, thereby fostering better decision-making and supporting circular economy objectives [3].

The Battery Passport is designed to house an extensive array of data attributes, ensuring a holistic view of each battery. These attributes include, but are not limited to, the following key categories [3] [7]:

  • General Battery & Manufacturer Information: This foundational section includes basic identification details such as the battery's unique serial number, its type, model, and nominal capacity, along with comprehensive administrative information about the manufacturer responsible for its production and placement on the market.
  • Carbon Footprint: This critical section will store the declared carbon footprint values, expressed in kg CO2e per kWh, along with the assigned carbon footprint performance class (e.g., Class A, B, C). It will also include a link to the public version of the underlying carbon footprint study.
  • Supply Chain Due Diligence: Information pertaining to the ethical and responsible sourcing of raw materials, including details on due diligence policies and practices implemented to address social and environmental risks in the supply chain. This may include data on conflict minerals and human rights.
  • Materials & Composition: Detailed information on the battery's chemical composition, including the specific active materials used in the anode and cathode, electrolyte type, and the presence and concentration of critical raw materials such as lithium, cobalt, nickel, and manganese.
  • Circularity & Resource Efficiency: Data points designed to support circular economy principles, including information on the battery's design for durability, repairability, ease of disassembly, and the minimum percentage of recycled content (e.g., cobalt, lead, lithium, nickel) present in the battery. This section will also provide guidance on reuse, repurposing, and recycling processes.
  • Performance & Durability: Key electrochemical performance metrics, such as nominal voltage, energy density, power capability, expected cycle life, and degradation rates. This information is vital for assessing the battery's fitness for purpose and its potential for second-life applications.
  • Traceability Information: Beyond the unique serial number, this includes other identifiers that link the physical battery to its digital record, potentially incorporating batch numbers, production dates, and manufacturing plant codes. This ensures end-to-end traceability throughout the battery's lifecycle.

6. The Role of SHA-256 Hashing in Battery Passport Integrity

The integrity and immutability of the vast amounts of data contained within Digital Product Passports, and specifically the Battery Passport, are of paramount importance for building trust and ensuring regulatory compliance. While not always explicitly detailed in every public summary of the regulation, the underlying technical mechanisms to achieve this are crucial. Here, SHA-256 hashing emerges as a critical technology for safeguarding data integrity. SHA-256, which stands for Secure Hash Algorithm 256-bit, is a widely recognized cryptographic hash function. Its primary function is to take an input (or 'message') of any size and produce a unique, fixed-size 256-bit (32-byte) hash value, often referred to as a 'digital fingerprint' [8].

The power of SHA-256 lies in its deterministic nature and its extreme sensitivity to input changes. Even the slightest alteration—a single character change—in the original data will result in a drastically different SHA-256 hash value. This characteristic makes it an exceptionally effective tool for detecting any form of tampering or unauthorized modifications to the data. In the practical application of the Battery Passport, SHA-256 hashing can be employed to generate a unique digital fingerprint for the battery's data at various critical junctures throughout its lifecycle. For instance, a hash could be generated at the point of manufacturing, at the time of carbon footprint declaration, or when significant maintenance or repurposing occurs. This hash can then be securely stored on a blockchain or another form of distributed ledger technology. By doing so, an immutable and verifiable record is created. Any subsequent attempt to alter the battery's data in the passport would immediately be revealed by a mismatch between the current data's hash and the recorded hash, thereby flagging the data as compromised. This robust mechanism significantly enhances trust and transparency among all stakeholders—from original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) and recyclers to regulatory bodies and end-users—by providing an undeniable assurance that the information presented in the Battery Passport is authentic, reliable, and has not been tampered with [8].

7. Impact on Global Exporters: South African PGM and Lithium Exporters

The extraterritorial reach of the EU Battery Regulation is a critical consideration for global trade. The regulation explicitly states its applicability to all batteries placed on the EU market, regardless of their country of origin [3]. This fundamental principle means that manufacturers and other actors within the supply chain located outside the European Union are directly impacted if their products, or components thereof, are destined for the lucrative European market [3] [5]. For nations like South Africa, which possess significant reserves and are major global exporters of critical raw materials such as platinum group metals (PGMs) and lithium—both indispensable for the production of advanced batteries—compliance with this regulation is not merely an option but an absolute prerequisite for maintaining continued market access and competitiveness within the EU [5].

South African PGM and lithium exporters, along with other global suppliers, will face heightened demands for transparency and data provision throughout their supply chains. They will be required to furnish comprehensive and verifiable data necessary for the accurate calculation of carbon footprints and for fulfilling stringent due diligence requirements. This includes meticulous details concerning raw material extraction processes, subsequent processing activities, and the environmental impact of transportation. The regulation's emphasis on the use of primary (company-specific) data for key lifecycle stages, particularly for core battery components, will necessitate the establishment of sophisticated and robust data collection systems at the source of these raw materials. Furthermore, it will demand unprecedented levels of transparent collaboration and data exchange with downstream battery manufacturers. Failure to adequately comply with these evolving regulatory demands could lead to significant market access restrictions, potentially disrupting established trade relationships and undermining economic opportunities for these exporting nations [5]. Therefore, proactive engagement and investment in compliance infrastructure are essential for global exporters to navigate this new regulatory landscape successfully.

8. The Battery Regulation Timeline: 2024 Transitional Phase, 2025 Carbon Footprint Declarations, 2027 Performance Classes

The implementation of the EU Battery Regulation is characterized by a carefully orchestrated phased approach, designed to provide economic operators with a structured timeline to adapt to the new requirements. This gradual rollout involves several key dates for the introduction of different obligations and for various battery categories, allowing industries to progressively align their processes and systems with the stringent new standards [2] [4] [5].

Here is a detailed summary of the key milestones and their implications:

Date Requirement Affected Battery Categories
17 August 2023 Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 officially enters into force, marking the beginning of the new regulatory era for batteries in the EU. All batteries
18 February 2024 Initial provisions of the regulation take effect, laying the groundwork for subsequent, more specific requirements. All batteries
18 August 2024 The CE marking becomes mandatory for all batteries placed on the EU market, certifying their compliance with EU safety, health, and environmental protection requirements. All batteries
18 February 2025 The Carbon Footprint Declaration becomes mandatory for Electric Vehicle (EV) batteries. This date may be deferred by 12 months after the entry into force of the relevant delegated act specifying the calculation methodology. EV batteries
18 August 2025 Directive 2006/66/EC is fully repealed, making Regulation (EU) 2023/1542 the sole legal framework. Additionally, new end-of-life management rules for batteries take effect. All batteries
18 February 2026 The Carbon Footprint Declaration becomes mandatory for rechargeable industrial batteries with a capacity greater than 2 kWh. This date may be deferred by 18 months after the entry into force of the relevant delegated act. Rechargeable industrial batteries >2kWh
18 February 2027 The Digital Battery Passport becomes mandatory for EV batteries and rechargeable industrial batteries (>2kWh). Removability and replaceability requirements for portable and LMT batteries also come into force. From this date, the carbon footprint declaration must be accessible via the Battery Passport's QR code. EV batteries, rechargeable industrial batteries >2kWh, LMT batteries
18 August 2027 Specific performance and durability requirements for industrial rechargeable batteries come into effect, aiming to ensure consistent efficiency and longevity. Industrial rechargeable batteries
18 August 2028 The Carbon Footprint Declaration becomes mandatory for Light Means of Transport (LMT) batteries (or 18 months after the delegated act). Minimum recycled material content requirements are introduced for industrial rechargeable batteries (>2kWh), EV batteries, and SLI batteries. Performance and durability standards for portable batteries also become mandatory. LMT batteries, industrial rechargeable batteries >2kWh, EV batteries, SLI batteries, portable batteries
31 December 2030 The European Commission is mandated to assess the feasibility of extending carbon footprint requirements to portable batteries and industrial batteries with a capacity of 2 kWh or less. Portable batteries, industrial batteries ≤2kWh

9. Comparison Table: Battery Type vs. DPP Deadline vs. Carbon Footprint Requirement

To provide a clear and concise overview of the varying requirements and deadlines across different battery types, the following comparison table summarizes the key obligations under the EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542:

Battery Type Digital Product Passport (DPP) Deadline Carbon Footprint (CF) Declaration Requirement CF Performance Classes Requirement CF Maximum Threshold Requirement
Electric Vehicle (EV) Batteries 18 February 2027 18 February 2025 (or 12 months after delegated act) To be determined by delegated acts (expected after CF declaration) To be determined by delegated acts (expected after performance classes)
Rechargeable Industrial Batteries (>2kWh) 18 February 2027 18 February 2026 (or 18 months after delegated act) To be determined by delegated acts (expected after CF declaration) To be determined by delegated acts (expected after performance classes)
Light Means of Transport (LMT) Batteries 18 February 2027 18 August 2028 (or 18 months after delegated act) To be determined by delegated acts (expected after CF declaration) To be determined by delegated acts (expected after performance classes)
Portable Batteries Not currently required Not currently required (assessment by 31 December 2030) Not currently required Not currently required
SLI Batteries Not currently required Not currently required Not currently required Not currently required

Conclusion

The EU Battery Regulation 2023/1542 represents a significant step towards a more sustainable and transparent battery value chain. The mandatory carbon footprint declaration and the introduction of the Digital Battery Passport will fundamentally reshape how batteries are manufactured, traded, and recycled globally. For manufacturers and exporters worldwide, understanding and proactively preparing for these requirements is not merely a matter of compliance but a strategic imperative for maintaining market access and competitiveness in the evolving European landscape. The emphasis on robust data collection, transparent reporting, and third-party verification underscores the EU's commitment to verifiable environmental claims and a truly circular economy.
